content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers Chapter 6

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 6:2 -- "makes an extraordinary vow" vs. "makes a special vow" vs. "makes a special vow" (yafli' lindor neder)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "when he-makes-extraordinary to-vow a-vow of a-Nazirite" |
| LOGOS | "makes a special vow, the vow of a Nazirite" |
| PNEUMA | "makes a special vow -- the vow of a Nazirite" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "makes an extraordinary vow" -- the Hebrew yafli' is the hiphil of pala' ("to be wonderful/extraordinary"), a verb denoting something beyond the ordinary. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "special," which is adequate but flattens the force of pala'. The LXX renders megalos euxetai ("greatly vows"), confirming the intensifying sense. "Extraordinary" preserves the root's semantic force -- this is not merely "special" but wondrous, an act of exceptional devotion. EMET's instinct to preserve the pala' force is correct; the consensus elevates "special" to "extraordinary."

---

### 6:2 -- "to separate himself" vs. "to set themselves apart" (le-hazzir)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to-separate-himself to-YHWH" |
| LOGOS | "to separate himself to YHWH" |
| PNEUMA | "to set themselves apart for YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "to separate himself to YHWH" -- EMET and LOGOS agree on "separate himself," which directly renders the hiphil infinitive le-hazzir from the root nzr ("to separate"). PNEUMA's "set themselves apart" uses a more dynamic equivalent but introduces plural pronoun ("themselves") to avoid gendered language -- a choice not supported by the Hebrew singular masculine form (which serves as generic in legal contexts). The preposition "to" (le-) rather than "for" preserves the directional sense of dedication toward YHWH. LOGOS is followed.

---

### 6:3 -- pronoun number: "he shall" vs. "they must" throughout vv. 3–8
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he shall separate himself" (singular masculine throughout) |
| LOGOS | "he shall separate himself" (singular masculine throughout) |
| PNEUMA | "they must abstain" (plural throughout) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "he shall separate himself" (singular) -- the Hebrew text uses masculine singular verb forms throughout vv. 3–8 (yazzir, yishteh, yo'khal, etc.), following the generic masculine singular established by the casuistic formula in v. 2 (ish o ishah, "a man or a woman"). EMET and LOGOS preserve this grammatical number faithfully. PNEUMA shifts to plural "they" for gender-inclusive English, but the binding convention is to follow the Hebrew grammatical forms closely. The singular is retained; the verse 2 formula already establishes that both men and women are included.

---

### 6:4 -- "is made from the grapevine" vs. "is produced from the grapevine" vs. "comes from the grapevine" (ye'aseh mi-gefen ha-yayin)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "which is-made from-the-vine of the-wine" |
| LOGOS | "that is produced from the grapevine" |
| PNEUMA | "that comes from the grapevine" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "that is made from the grapevine" -- the Hebrew ye'aseh is a niphal ("is made/done"), a passive construction. EMET and LOGOS preserve the passive voice; PNEUMA's "comes from" shifts to active/intransitive. "Made" is a more literal rendering of 'asah than "produced," and preserves the direct Hebrew sense. The construct gefen ha-yayin ("vine of the wine") is rendered as "grapevine" by all three translators, which is the accepted equivalent.

---

### 6:5 -- "grow freely" vs. "grow long" vs. "grow long and free" (gadel pera' se'ar ro'sho)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he-shall-let-grow-freely the-locks of the-hair of his-head" |
| LOGOS | "he shall let the locks of the hair of his head grow long" |
| PNEUMA | "they must let the hair of their head grow long and free" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "he shall let the locks of the hair of his head grow freely" -- the Hebrew pera' means "loose, unbound, wild" hair, not specifically "long." The verb gadel means "to let grow great/large." EMET's "grow freely" captures the sense of pera' (unrestrained, unchecked growth). LOGOS's "grow long" is a reasonable result of uncut hair but substitutes an effect for the Hebrew description. PNEUMA combines both ("long and free") but loses the key word "locks" (pera' in its noun form). The consensus retains "locks" (from EMET/LOGOS) and "freely" (from EMET), capturing both the technical term and its force.

---

### 6:6 -- "come near to" vs. "come upon" vs. "go near" (lo' yavo')
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "upon a-soul of a-dead-one he-shall-not come" |
| LOGOS | "he shall not come near to a dead person" |
| PNEUMA | "they must not go near a dead body" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "he shall not come near to a dead person" -- the Hebrew yavo' ("he shall come") with al ("upon") and nefesh met ("a soul of a dead one") is rendered by LOGOS with the supplied "near to," which correctly communicates the prohibition against approaching a corpse. EMET preserves the raw "upon a soul of a dead one" with its literal nefesh, but "dead person" (LOGOS) is the standard English equivalent of nefesh met. PNEUMA's "dead body" is also acceptable but "person" better renders nefesh. LOGOS is followed.

---

### 6:7 -- "consecration of his God" vs. "sign of their dedication to God" (nezer Elohav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-consecration of his-God" |
| LOGOS | "the consecration of his God" |
| PNEUMA | "the sign of their dedication to God" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the consecration of his God" -- the Hebrew nezer Elohav means "the crown/consecration of his God." EMET and LOGOS render nezer as "consecration," which captures the primary sense in this context (the state of being set apart). PNEUMA adds "sign of" as an interpretive gloss -- the hair is indeed the sign of the nezer, but the text says the nezer itself is upon his head, not "the sign of" it. PNEUMA also substitutes "dedication" for "consecration" and drops the possessive pronoun from Elohav ("his God"). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS exactly.

---

### 6:9 -- "very suddenly beside him" vs. "in suddenness suddenly upon him" vs. "suddenly dies in their presence" (yamut met alav be-feta' pit'om)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "dies a-dead-one upon-him in-suddenness suddenly" |
| LOGOS | "anyone dies very suddenly beside him" |
| PNEUMA | "someone suddenly dies in their presence" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "anyone dies very suddenly beside him" -- the Hebrew doubled expression be-feta' pit'om emphasizes involuntary, sudden occurrence. LOGOS captures the doubling with "very suddenly," which preserves the intensifying force. EMET's "in-suddenness suddenly" is too literal for readable English. PNEUMA's single "suddenly" loses the doubled emphasis. "Beside him" (LOGOS) for alav ("upon him") is a reasonable rendering of the spatial sense; "in their presence" (PNEUMA) adds interpretive specificity. LOGOS is followed.

---

### 6:11 -- "sin offering" vs. "purification offering" (khattat)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "sin-offering" |
| LOGOS | "sin offering" |
| PNEUMA | "purification offering" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "sin offering" -- the Hebrew khattat derives from the root khata' ("to sin/miss the mark"). The traditional rendering "sin offering" is used by both EMET and LOGOS and is the established English equivalent throughout the Pentateuch. PNEUMA's "purification offering" reflects the scholarly argument (Milgrom) that the khattat primarily purifies the sanctuary rather than atoning for sin. While this is a defensible interpretation, "sin offering" preserves the etymological connection to the root khata' and is the term used consistently in this translation project. The same verse uses the verb khata' ("he sinned"), making the sin-offering/sinned connection explicit in the Hebrew.

---

### 6:11 -- "because he sinned on account of the dead person" vs. "because they incurred guilt through contact with the dead" (me-asher khata' al ha-nafesh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "from-that-which he-sinned upon the-soul" |
| LOGOS | "because he sinned on account of the dead person" |
| PNEUMA | "because they incurred guilt through contact with the dead" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "because he sinned on account of the dead person" -- the Hebrew me-asher khata' al ha-nafesh literally reads "from that which he sinned upon/concerning the dead person." LOGOS preserves both the verb "sinned" (khata') and the referent "the dead person" (ha-nafesh) with appropriate English phrasing. PNEUMA replaces "sinned" with "incurred guilt" (an interpretation, not a translation of khata') and adds "through contact with" (not in the Hebrew). EMET's "upon the-soul" retains the literal nefesh but is opaque. LOGOS is followed.

---

### 6:12 -- "the former days shall fall away" vs. "the previous days do not count" (ve-ha-yamim ha-rishonim yippelu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-the-days the-first shall-fall" |
| LOGOS | "the former days shall fall away" |
| PNEUMA | "the previous days do not count" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the former days shall fall away" -- the Hebrew yippelu ("shall fall") is a vivid metaphor: the days collapse, become void. LOGOS preserves the verb "fall" with the clarifying adverb "away." PNEUMA's "do not count" conveys the legal meaning but obliterates the Hebrew image entirely, and the LXX's alogoi esontai ("shall be without account") is itself already an interpretive rendering of the MT's more concrete metaphor. EMET's bare "shall fall" is too terse. The consensus follows LOGOS, retaining the conjunction "And" per binding convention.

---

### 6:13 -- "he shall be brought" vs. "he shall bring himself" (yavi' oto)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he-shall-bring him" |
| LOGOS | "he shall be brought" |
| PNEUMA | "they are to be brought" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "he shall be brought" -- the Hebrew yavi' oto ("he shall bring him") is ambiguous: it may be reflexive ("he shall bring himself") or impersonal passive ("one shall bring him" = "he shall be brought"). LOGOS renders the impersonal passive, which suits the formal context of a presentation at the sanctuary. PNEUMA agrees on the passive but uses plural "they." EMET preserves the literal "he shall bring him" but leaves the ambiguity unresolved for the English reader. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 6:14 -- "peace offerings" (plural) vs. "peace offering" (singular) (li-shelamim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "for-peace-offerings" |
| LOGOS | "as a peace offering" |
| PNEUMA | "as a peace offering" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "as peace offerings" -- the Hebrew shelamim is a plural form (literally "peaces/wholenesses"), and this plurality is consistent throughout the Pentateuch. While English convention sometimes singularizes it, the Hebrew plural should be preserved for consistency with the translation's formal approach, as EMET does. The consensus uses the plural without the hyphen.

---

### 6:19 -- "palms" vs. "hands" (kappei)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "upon the-palms of the-Nazirite" |
| LOGOS | "upon the hands of the Nazirite" |
| PNEUMA | "in the hands of the Nazirite" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "upon the palms of the Nazirite" -- the Hebrew kappei (dual construct of kaf, "palm/hollow of the hand") specifically denotes the open palms, not the hands generally (which would be yadei). The ritual gesture requires the items to be placed upon the upturned, open palms. EMET preserves the anatomical precision. LOGOS and PNEUMA both generalize to "hands." The consensus follows EMET, as the distinction matters for the ritual description.

---

### 6:20 -- "It is holy" vs. "This is a holy portion" (qodesh hu')
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "holy it [is] for-the-priest" |
| LOGOS | "It is holy for the priest" |
| PNEUMA | "This is a holy portion belonging to the priest" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "It is holy for the priest" -- the Hebrew qodesh hu' la-kohen ("holy it [is] for the priest") is a terse declaration. LOGOS preserves the structure exactly. PNEUMA expands with "a holy portion belonging to," adding words not in the Hebrew. EMET's word order is awkward in English. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 6:21 -- "besides what his hand can attain" vs. "in addition to whatever else they can afford" (mi-levad asher tassig yado)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "apart-from that-which his-hand attains" |
| LOGOS | "besides what his hand can attain" |
| PNEUMA | "in addition to whatever else they can afford" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "besides what his hand can attain" -- the Hebrew idiom tassig yado ("his hand reaches/attains") is the standard expression for financial capacity. LOGOS preserves both the idiom ("his hand") and the verb ("attain") while being readable. EMET is essentially equivalent. PNEUMA replaces the idiom with "they can afford," which is the meaning but loses the distinctive Hebrew phrasing. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 6:23 -- "saying to them" vs. "say to them" (amor lahem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "say to-them" |
| LOGOS | "saying to them" |
| PNEUMA | "Say to them" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "say to them" -- the Hebrew amor is an infinitive absolute used as an imperative, giving the force of a direct command ("Say!"). EMET and PNEUMA render it as an imperative ("say"), which captures the commanding force. LOGOS renders it as a participle ("saying"), which softens the imperative into a participial continuation of the previous clause. The infinitive absolute here is more forceful than a mere gerund. The consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA with the imperative "say."

---

### 6:24 -- "May YHWH bless you and keep you" vs. "YHWH bless you and keep you" vs. "May YHWH bless you and protect you" (yevarekh-kha YHWH ve-yishmerekha)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "May-bless-you YHWH and-may-he-keep-you" |
| LOGOS | "YHWH bless you and keep you" |
| PNEUMA | "May YHWH bless you and protect you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "May YHWH bless you and keep you" -- the Hebrew uses jussive verb forms (yevarekh-kha, "may he bless you"), which express a wish or prayer. The "May" rendering (EMET, PNEUMA) makes the optative force explicit in English. LOGOS omits "May," creating a more archaic, liturgical cadence ("YHWH bless you"), which is also valid for jussive forms in English. The consensus includes "May" to mark the jussive clearly. For the second verb, ve-yishmerekha ("and may he keep you"), EMET and LOGOS use "keep" (the primary sense of shamar), while PNEUMA uses "protect." "Keep" is the traditional rendering that preserves the broader semantic range of shamar (to guard, to watch over, to preserve, to be faithful to), whereas "protect" narrows the meaning. The consensus uses "keep."

---

### 6:25 -- "be gracious to you" vs. "show you grace" (vi-ykhunnekka)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-may-he-be-gracious-to-you" |
| LOGOS | "and be gracious to you" |
| PNEUMA | "and show you grace" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and be gracious to you" -- the Hebrew vi-ykhunnekka is the jussive of khanan ("to show grace/favor"). EMET and LOGOS both render "be gracious to you," which preserves the adjectival/stative sense of the verb (YHWH's character oriented toward the recipient). PNEUMA's "show you grace" is an active construction that makes grace an object shown rather than a disposition embodied. The traditional rendering "be gracious to you" has deep liturgical resonance and follows the Hebrew structure more closely. LOGOS is followed.

---

### 6:26 -- "upon you" vs. "toward you" (eleikha)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "unto-you" |
| LOGOS | "upon you" |
| PNEUMA | "toward you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "upon you" -- the Hebrew eleikha means "to you / toward you / upon you." In v. 25, the same preposition is used for the shining of the face, and in v. 26 for the lifting of the face. LOGOS's "upon" in v. 26 captures the sense of the divine gaze resting on the person -- the face is lifted and turned upon them, bestowing attention. PNEUMA's "toward" suggests direction without arrival. The consensus follows LOGOS: "upon you."

---

### 6:26 -- "give you peace" vs. "set for you peace" (ve-yasem lekha shalom)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-may-he-set for-you peace" |
| LOGOS | "and give you peace" |
| PNEUMA | "and give you peace" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and give you peace" -- the Hebrew ve-yasem ("and may he set/place") literally means "to put/place," not "to give." EMET preserves the literal "set for you," which has a distinctive force: shalom is placed upon the person like a tangible reality. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "give," which is the natural English equivalent for the idiom of bestowing peace. While "set" is more literal, "give you peace" has overwhelming liturgical tradition behind it and is the rendering that will resonate with the blessing's universal reception. The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA, though EMET's note on the "setting/placing" nuance is valuable.

---

### 6:27 -- "And they shall put My name" vs. "In this way they will place my name" (ve-samu et shemi)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-they-shall-put my-name upon the-Sons of Israel" |
| LOGOS | "And they shall put My name upon the Sons of Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "In this way they will place my name upon the Sons of Israel" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they shall put My name upon the Sons of Israel" -- EMET and LOGOS agree on the conjunction "And" plus "shall put," which faithfully renders ve-samu (vav-consecutive + qal perfect of sum, "to put/place"). PNEUMA adds "In this way" to connect v. 27 to the preceding blessing, but this phrase is not in the Hebrew. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the conjunction "And" per binding convention and using "put" for sum.

---

### 6:27 -- "I Myself will bless them" vs. "I shall bless them" (va-ani avarakhem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-I shall-bless-them" |
| LOGOS | "I Myself will bless them" |
| PNEUMA | "I myself will bless them" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "I Myself will bless them" -- the Hebrew va-ani avarakhem features the emphatic independent pronoun ani ("I") before the verb, creating a strong assertion of divine agency. LOGOS and PNEUMA both capture the emphasis with "I Myself/myself." EMET's "and-I" does not sufficiently convey the emphatic force. The consensus follows LOGOS with the capitalized "Myself" to mark the divine self-reference, consistent with the capitalization of "My name" earlier in the verse.
